examples.title: Basic Tokens, Data Types, and Variable Declaration
examples.description: Demonstrates fundamental C tokens (keywords, identifiers, operators), basic data types (int, float, char), and variable declaration with initialization.
examples.explanation:
1. Comments are ignored by the compiler (tokens not executed)
2. Keywords are reserved words with special meaning (int, float, char, return)
3. Identifiers are user-defined names for variables (age, price, grade)
4. %d, %f, %c are format specifier tokens used in printf
5. Each variable has a specific data type that determines its memory allocation

examples.title: Type Casting and Constants
examples.description: Shows implicit and explicit type casting (tokens for operators), constant variables using const and #define, and sizeof operator.
examples.explanation:
1. #define is a preprocessor directive token (macro substitution)
2. Implicit casting: when int is assigned to float, automatic conversion occurs
3. Explicit casting using (float) forces conversion before operation
4. const makes a variable read-only; attempting to modify causes compilation error
5. sizeof is a compile-time operator that returns the size in bytes
6. Each data type has different memory requirements in C

examples.title: Enumerated Types and Typedef
examples.description: Demonstrates advanced token usage with enum (enumerated type), typedef for creating custom type names, and variable scope.
examples.explanation:
1. typedef creates alternative names for existing types (uint = unsigned int)
2. enum defines a set of named integer constants starting from 0 (or specified value)
3. struct groups different data types together; typedef simplifies usage
4. Variables have different scopes: global (file-wide), local (function), block ( braces)
5. Token types demonstrated: keywords (typedef, enum, struct, int, float), identifiers, operators, separators (braces, parentheses, semicolons)
6. C tokens are the smallest building blocks: identifiers, keywords, constants, operators, separators
